title: Solving Inverse Wave Problems Using Spacetime Radial Basis Functions in Neural Networks

research.question: Can spacetime radial basis functions strengthen a physics-informed neural network enough to reconstruct two-dimensional wave fields from final-time observations when boundary or initial data are incomplete and observations are noisy?
research.system: A transient two-dimensional wave equation on an irregular star-shaped spatial domain, represented as a three-dimensional spacetime point cloud with boundary, initial, final-time, residual, and RBF source points.
research.inputData: Synthetic values from the paper's analytical wave solution, sampled for four inverse cases with different boundary and initial-condition availability; a second set adds Gaussian noise at level s = 0.1.
research.method: Represent spacetime coordinates with simplified MQ, IMQ, or Gaussian radial basis functions, feed those features to a multilayer perceptron, constrain training with data and wave-equation residual losses, tune the architecture through Bayesian optimization, and solve the inverse cases with the selected configuration.
research.benchmark: The study checks the forward solution against the analytical field, compares three RBF families, collocation-point quantities, dilation parameters, optimizers, activation functions, random forest, support vector machine, and four inverse-data scenarios.
research.uncertainty: Robustness is tested with deliberately omitted initial or boundary conditions and additive noise at s = 0.1. The results are numerical experiments on one prescribed geometry and solution; they do not quantify model-form, geometry, or field-data uncertainty.
research.conclusion: The selected spacetime IMQ RBF-PINN accurately reconstructs the tested inverse wave fields, retains reported RMSE of order 10^-4 under s = 0.1 noise, and remains usable with incomplete boundary information within the synthetic cases studied.

narrative.throughline: Turn a sparsely observed wave movie into a spacetime geometry, encode distance to source points as RBF features, enforce the wave equation during learning, and return the inferred field to the original irregular domain.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: A viewer who understands that a wave equation propagates displacement through space but has not seen inverse problems or physics-informed neural networks.
narrative.stakes: Inverse wave reconstruction is ill-posed, and conventional discretizations can be costly or sensitive when observations and boundary conditions are incomplete.
narrative.resolution: The paper's optimized IMQ RBF-PINN reconstructs the four synthetic inverse cases and visibly preserves the spatial wave pattern under missing conditions and imposed noise, with limits stated by the experiment design.

background.premise: Inverse wave problems are difficult because ill-posedness, computational demand, and discretization error act together.
background.visibleEvidence: Three obstacles surround an incomplete wave field: non-uniqueness, a dense computational grid, and accumulated discretization error.
background.whyNow: The paper motivates a meshless, physics-constrained reconstruction before introducing its network.
background.boundary: This is the paper's general motivation, not a measured comparison for every conventional inverse solver.
background.sourceExcerpt: inverse wave problems struggle with ill- posedness, significant computational demands, and discretization
background.visualObject: An irregular wave domain with missing observations beside a deforming mesh and multiple candidate past fields
background.motionPurpose: Expose why the missing past cannot be recovered by visual interpolation alone
background.stepDetail: Show the same sparse observations feeding several plausible fields, then add the cost and discretization constraints.
background.transitionBridge: A governing equation can narrow those plausible fields.

background.premise: A PINN constrains a neural approximation by placing the wave-equation residual inside training.
background.visibleEvidence: A candidate field enters a differential-operator block and a residual gauge falls only when the field follows the wave equation.
background.whyNow: This establishes the physical constraint that distinguishes the method from an unconstrained regressor.
background.boundary: A small residual enforces the stated PDE at sampled points; it does not by itself prove uniqueness or physical validity outside the modeled conditions.
background.sourceExcerpt: equation, represented as residuals, into the network training process as physical constraints.
background.visualObject: A neural wave surface connected to residual samples distributed through spacetime
background.motionPurpose: Make the PDE constraint visible as a penalty on nonphysical curvature and acceleration
background.stepDetail: Pulse residual points where the trial surface violates Equation (8), then dim them as training reduces the mismatch.
background.transitionBridge: The network still needs features that express where each spacetime sample sits relative to source points.

background.premise: Spacetime RBFs provide the meshless geometric representation used by the PINN to solve the wave equation.
background.visibleEvidence: Time folds upward from the spatial domain to form a spacetime volume populated by RBF source centers.
background.whyNow: The integration of spacetime RBFs with a PINN is the paper's central methodological contribution.
background.boundary: The paper evaluates three simplified RBF choices on its test problem; it does not establish universal superiority for all wave equations.
background.sourceExcerpt: study integrates spacetime RBFs with a PINN to solve the wave equation.
background.visualObject: A star-shaped domain extruded along time with radial influence shells around source points
background.motionPurpose: Transform abstract input coordinates into a geometry the network can compare by distance
background.stepDetail: Extrude the spatial outline into time and reveal overlapping RBF neighborhoods across the volume.
background.transitionBridge: The meshless representation now receives measured spacetime points in M01's inverse-parameter formulation.

methods.input: Measurements taken at specified spatial and temporal points
methods.output: Wave speed material properties or source location designated as unknown characteristics
methods.validity: Section 2.1 states this general parameter-inference scope; Section 4 later narrows the application to reconstruction of a prescribed past wave distribution.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.1, inverse-wave-problem paragraph immediately preceding Equation (4)
methods.sourceExcerpt: Inverse wave problems aim to infer unknown parameters, such as wave speed, ma- terial properties, or source location, based on measurements taken at specific spatial and temporal points [40].
methods.visualObject: Measured spacetime points connected to unknown wave-speed material-property and source-location markers
methods.motionPurpose: Expose the general inverse quantities defined by the methodology before the application narrows the target
methods.stepDetail: Place measured spacetime points; mark wave speed material properties and source location as candidate unknowns.
methods.transitionBridge: Represent each observation and source center in one spacetime coordinate system.

methods.input: Target coordinate x = (x, y, t) and spacetime source coordinate x_i^s = (x_i^s, y_i^s, t_i^s)
methods.output: Distance r_i from every target point to every source point in the combined spacetime domain
methods.validity: Time is treated as an additional coordinate in the paper's numerical representation; its scaling must remain consistent with the prescribed formulation.
methods.locator: PDF pages 4-5, Section 2.2, Equation (10) and following spacetime-distance and source-point definitions
methods.sourceExcerpt: M represents the complete count of source points, λj represents the coefficient that needs to be determined, ri expressed as ri = x − xis is the spacetime distance, xis defined as xis = xis, yis, tis represents the source point
methods.visualObject: Distance rays joining one target point to many source centers in the spacetime volume
methods.motionPurpose: Reveal the scalar geometry that drives every RBF feature
methods.stepDetail: Select one target point and extend labeled r_i rays to all source points.
methods.transitionBridge: Turn each distance into a radial feature value.

methods.input: Spacetime distances r_i and a selected simplified MQ, IMQ, or Gaussian basis
methods.output: Simplified MQ, IMQ, or Gaussian values phi(r_i) for every source point
methods.validity: The calculation uses one of the three simplified forms stated in Equations (11)-(13).
methods.sourceExcerpt: inverse multiquadric (IMQ), and simplified Gaussian functions. These can be formulated as follows:
methods.visualObject: Three radial response profiles converting source-point distances into basis values
methods.motionPurpose: Convert each geometric distance into one radial response value
methods.stepDetail: Apply the selected radial profile to each visible r_i ray.
methods.transitionBridge: Collect the calculated radial values at the network input.

methods.input: Calculated radial basis values phi(r_i) for all source points
methods.output: A source-point-width feature vector supplied to the multilayer perceptron
methods.validity: Figure 1 states that computed RBF values form the MLP input and that input width equals the source-point quantity.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The proposed architecture extends the capabilities of PINNs by incorporating the spacetime RBFs to enhance the preprocessing of input data. The architecture illustrated in Figure 1 integrates a spacetime RBF with a multilayer perceptron (MLP). The spacetime RBF is calculated using the formulas provided in Equations (11), (12), or (13). These computed RBF values are subsequently employed as the input data for the MLP. As a result, the input layer of the MLP must be designed with a number of neurons that align with the quantity of source points.
methods.visualObject: A column of calculated radial values aligned with a fixed-width neural input layer
methods.motionPurpose: Assemble the radial responses into the actual network input
methods.stepDetail: Stack the visible phi(r_i) values into one feature column.
methods.transitionBridge: The assembled feature vector becomes the input propagated through the MLP in M05.

methods.input: Source-point-width RBF feature vector at the MLP input layer
methods.output: MLP output-layer solution approximation
methods.validity: The stated MLP is fully connected from its input layer through hidden layers to an output layer.
methods.locator: PDF pages 6-7, Section 2.3, Figure 1 and MLP architecture paragraph
methods.sourceExcerpt: each neuron in a given layer is linked to all neurons in the next layer. Its architecture comprises an input layer, several hidden layers, and an output layer.
methods.visualObject: RBF feature values moving through fully connected hidden layers into one solution output
methods.motionPurpose: Make the omitted MLP forward operation explicit
methods.stepDetail: Feed the RBF feature vector into the input layer; carry activations through the connected hidden layers; reveal the output-layer approximation.
methods.transitionBridge: Compare the propagated solution approximation with observed values.

methods.input: Predicted values and observed values at initial and boundary data points
methods.output: Data-loss term L_data
methods.validity: The source defines L_data as the difference between model predictions and observed data.
methods.sourceExcerpt: where Ldata represents the data loss, which quantifies the difference between the model’s predictions and the observed data; Lphysics denotes the physics loss, which captures the residuals of the wave equations; and λR and λP denote the weight values.
methods.visualObject: Prediction and observation markers connected by mismatch segments that collect into L_data
methods.motionPurpose: Turn visible prediction-observation differences into the data-loss term
methods.stepDetail: Connect each prediction to its observation and collect the visible differences into L_data.
methods.transitionBridge: Measure the governing-equation mismatch at residual points.

methods.input: Wave-equation residual values R(x, y, t) at residual points
methods.output: Mean-squared residual loss L_physics
methods.validity: Equation (17) defines the physics loss as the mean squared wave-equation residual.
methods.sourceExcerpt: physics-based loss (residuals of the wave equation). This study uses the mean squared error as the loss function.
methods.visualObject: Residual markers collapsing into a mean-squared physics-loss gauge
methods.motionPurpose: Turn local PDE violations into one physics-loss scalar
methods.stepDetail: Square the visible residual values and average them into L_physics.
methods.transitionBridge: Place the data and physics losses on a weighted balance.

methods.input: Data loss Ldata and physics loss Lphysics
methods.output: Objective Loss = lambda_R Ldata + lambda_P Lphysics
methods.validity: Equation (15) defines the objective with weights lambda_R and lambda_P for Ldata and Lphysics.
methods.sourceExcerpt: A general form of the loss function for a PINN is: Loss = λR Ldata + λP Lphysics, (15)
methods.visualObject: Data and physics loss scalars entering a weighted balance
methods.motionPurpose: Show how measured agreement and physical agreement jointly define training
methods.stepDetail: Weight the two visible loss scalars and merge them into the objective.
methods.transitionBridge: The RBF family must be fixed before the final architecture is tuned.

methods.input: Forward-case predictions from simplified MQ, IMQ, and Gaussian RBF-PINNs
methods.output: Simplified IMQ basis retained for subsequent analyses
methods.validity: The selection is empirical for the paper's analytical test field, point layout, and reported error metrics.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Among the tested RBFs, the simplified IMQ exhibits the most effective results.
methods.visualObject: Three aligned prediction-error strips labeled MQ, IMQ, and Gaussian
methods.motionPurpose: Make model selection a direct comparison rather than an unexplained choice
methods.stepDetail: Sweep across the three error strips and retain the IMQ strip with the reported strongest metrics.
methods.transitionBridge: With the basis fixed, determine a sufficient sampling layout.

methods.input: Candidate boundary-point quantities with 120 source points held constant
methods.output: A retained boundary-point quantity of 1002
methods.validity: The retained quantity is the empirical optimum for the paper's validation case rather than a general sampling theorem.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Based on the results of the evaluation of the impact of distribution point quantity on ac- curacy in this case, the optimal boundary point and source point quantities are deter- Mathematics 2025, 13, 725 mined to be 1002 and 69, respectively.
methods.visualObject: A star-shaped boundary gaining perimeter samples beside a fixed source-point set
methods.motionPurpose: Connect reported accuracy to the tested boundary-point quantity
methods.stepDetail: Sweep the boundary-point count through the tested values and retain 1002.
methods.transitionBridge: Hold the retained boundary layout while varying source points.

methods.input: Candidate RBF source-point quantities with the retained boundary layout
methods.output: A retained RBF source-point quantity of 69
methods.validity: The retained quantity is the empirical optimum for the paper's validation case rather than a general sampling theorem.
methods.locator: PDF page 11, Section 3.2, Figure 4(b); PDF page 19, Section 6, conclusion item (2)
methods.sourceExcerpt: the optimal quantities of boundary and source points are determined to be 1002 Mathematics 2025, 13, 725 19 of 21 and 69, respectively.
methods.visualObject: The retained star-shaped boundary beside a changing RBF source-point set
methods.motionPurpose: Connect reported accuracy to the tested source-point quantity
methods.stepDetail: Sweep the source-point count through the tested values and retain 69.
methods.transitionBridge: Tune the remaining network hyperparameters around the retained point layout.

methods.input: PINN architecture hyperparameters
methods.output: Selected hyperparameter configuration used in subsequent inverse analyses
methods.validity: The source uses Bayesian optimization to configure the PINN architecture and selects the resulting optimal configuration for further analyses.
methods.sourceExcerpt: These findings underscore the effectiveness of Bayesian optimization in configuring the PINN architecture, demonstrating its potential to enhance model accuracy and stability in addressing complex problems. The optimal hyperparameter configuration determined through this process is selected for further analyses.
methods.visualObject: A hyperparameter search trace converging on one highlighted network architecture
methods.motionPurpose: Show that architecture values are selected by an optimization procedure
methods.stepDetail: Move a trial marker through the bounded search space and lock onto the lowest validation error configuration.
methods.transitionBridge: The retained architecture and combined loss become the common input for three optimizer candidates.

methods.input: Selected network architecture and combined data-physics loss
methods.output: Network fitted to data and wave-equation constraints
methods.validity: The source trains the network by minimizing the combined loss so that it fits the data and satisfies the wave-equation constraints.
methods.locator: PDF page 7, Section 2.3, training paragraph following Equations (18)-(20)
methods.sourceExcerpt: The neural network is then trained by applying optimization methods to optimize the parameters and minimize the loss function. During training, this combined loss function is optimized to ensure that the network both fits the data and satisfies the essential physical constraints defined by the wave equation.
methods.visualObject: One configured network updating against data and wave-equation loss channels
methods.motionPurpose: Fit the configured network while retaining both source-stated loss channels
methods.stepDetail: Feed the combined loss to the configured network; update its parameters; retain the fitted field constrained by data and the wave equation.
methods.transitionBridge: The fitted network is duplicated only in the next scene for the reported optimizer comparison.

methods.input: Matched networks trained with LM, BFGS, and GD
methods.output: Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm selected for the proposed network
methods.validity: The comparison uses the paper's testing setup and metrics; it does not establish LM dominance for all PINNs or dataset sizes.
methods.sourceExcerpt: This study then examines the influence of different optimization functions on the accuracy of the spacetime IMQ RBF approach by evaluating three algorithms: LM, BFGS, and GD. The results demonstrate that the LM algorithm performs superior to the other two, achieving RMSE, NSE, MAE, and MRE values of 10−6, 1, 10−5, and 10−6, respectively, as listed in Table 2. Based on these findings, the proposed neural network employs the LM algorithm as its optimization method, ensuring optimal accuracy and performance.
methods.visualObject: Four metric columns comparing LM, BFGS, and GD
methods.motionPurpose: Tie the optimizer choice to the reported test metrics
methods.stepDetail: Reveal matched metric bars and retain LM as the paper's chosen training algorithm.
methods.transitionBridge: With LM retained, the same testing framework next compares the candidate activation functions.

methods.input: Testing-phase accuracy for matched sigmoid, ReLU, and Tanh network configurations
methods.output: Sigmoid activation selected for the forward and inverse wave networks
methods.validity: Table 3 reports RMSE, NSE, MRE, and MAE for the three activations and the source selects sigmoid for the study's networks.
methods.sourceExcerpt: This study conducts a comprehensive evaluation of the influence of different activation functions on model accuracy, specifically analyzing the performance of the sigmoid, ReLU, and Tanh functions. While all activation functions exhibit stable performance, sigmoid proves to be the most effective, yielding RMSE, NSE, MRE, and MAE values of 10−6, 1, 10−6, and 10−5, respectively, as listed in Table 3. Considering these findings, the sigmoid function is selected as the activation function for the neural networks employed in solving both forward and inverse wave problems in this study.
methods.visualObject: Testing metric columns for sigmoid, ReLU, and Tanh with sigmoid retained
methods.motionPurpose: Map the reported activation comparison and its selected activation
methods.stepDetail: Align the three activation metric columns; compare their reported values; retain sigmoid for the configured network.
methods.transitionBridge: The retained LM and sigmoid choices define the network whose weights are updated in M16.

methods.input: Combined data-physics loss and the LM-sigmoid network configuration
methods.output: Optimal weight parameters at the stated loss convergence criterion
methods.validity: The source states that optimization minimizes the combined loss until convergence determines the optimal weights.
methods.locator: PDF page 7, Section 2.3, training paragraph following Equations (18)-(20)
methods.sourceExcerpt: Upon meeting the convergence criteria, ε, for the loss function, the optimal weight parameters are determined.
methods.visualObject: Weight markers updating while data and physics loss gauges contract toward convergence
methods.motionPurpose: Make parameter training a distinct operation before any reconstructed field is shown
methods.stepDetail: Hold the combined loss visible; move the weight markers under the selected optimizer; stop at the stated convergence criterion.
methods.transitionBridge: Apply the trained network to each case's available observations.

methods.input: Available initial boundary and final-time observations for Cases A-D
methods.output: Reconstructed past wave fields for the four synthetic cases
methods.validity: The operation reconstructs prescribed synthetic fields on the paper's stated geometry under each case's available conditions.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The objective is to reconstruct the past data distribution based on present or final observations.
methods.visualObject: Four incomplete spacetime volumes filling with reconstructed wave surfaces
methods.motionPurpose: Show the model returning a past field from each case's available observations
methods.stepDetail: Hold each case's supplied observations fixed while its earlier wave slices appear.
methods.transitionBridge: Case A's reconstructed output joins RF and SVM outputs for the benchmark comparison in M18.

methods.input: Case A outputs from the proposed approach, random forest, and support vector machine
methods.output: Case A RMSE, MRE, and MAE comparison showing lower reported errors for the proposed approach
methods.validity: Table 5 limits this traditional-machine-learning benchmark to Case A and the paper's stated accuracy metrics.
methods.sourceExcerpt: In this study, we compare the performance of the proposed approach with random forest (RF) and support vector machine (SVM). Table 5 presents a comparative analysis of accuracy among RF, SVM, and the proposed approach for Case A. The proposed method achieves superior accuracy, with RMSE, MRE, and MAE values of 4.53 × 10−5, 1.69 × 10−2, and 1.57 × 10−4, respectively.
methods.visualObject: Three Case A benchmark rows for the proposed network, random forest, and support vector machine
methods.motionPurpose: Make the source-stated RF and SVM benchmark an explicit comparison operation
methods.stepDetail: Align the three methods; reveal their Case A RMSE, MRE, and MAE values; retain the reported comparison boundary.
methods.transitionBridge: The broader Cases A-D evaluation now separates RMSE from the other reported error metrics.

methods.input: Table 6 results for Cases A-D without noise
methods.output: Four without-noise RMSE values for Cases A-D
methods.validity: Table 6 reports one RMSE row across the four synthetic cases without noise.
methods.locator: PDF page 15, Section 4, Table 6, without-noise RMSE row
methods.visualObject: The without-noise RMSE row aligned under Cases A-D
methods.motionPurpose: Expose the four source-reported RMSE values without deriving an unreported calculation path
methods.stepDetail: Read the Table 6 RMSE row from Case A through Case D and retain its without-noise condition label.
methods.transitionBridge: Use the same casewise comparisons for the relative-error metric.

methods.input: Table 6 results for Cases A-D without noise
methods.output: Without-noise MRE values 2.10 × 10−3, 6.70 × 10−3, 1.67 × 10−1, and 2.09 × 10−2 for Cases A-D
methods.validity: Table 6 reports one MRE row across the four synthetic cases without noise.
methods.locator: PDF page 15, Section 4, Table 6, without-noise MRE row
methods.visualObject: The without-noise MRE row aligned under Cases A-D
methods.motionPurpose: Expose the four source-reported MRE values separately from the adjacent metric rows
methods.stepDetail: Read the Table 6 MRE row from Case A through Case D and retain its without-noise condition label.
methods.transitionBridge: Use the same casewise comparisons for the absolute-error metric.

methods.input: Table 6 results for Cases A-D without noise
methods.output: Without-noise MAE values 1.41 × 10−5, 3.52 × 10−5, 1.40 × 10−3, and 3.83 × 10−4 for Cases A-D
methods.validity: Table 6 reports one MAE row across the four synthetic cases without noise.
methods.locator: PDF page 15, Section 4, Table 6, without-noise MAE row
methods.visualObject: The without-noise MAE row aligned under Cases A-D
methods.motionPurpose: Expose the four source-reported MAE values without adding unsupported error-map meaning
methods.stepDetail: Read the Table 6 MAE row from Case A through Case D and retain its without-noise condition label.
methods.transitionBridge: Return the inferred surfaces to the original physical domain and state the experimental boundary.

symbols.meaning: Wave function reconstructed through space and time
symbols.unit: L in the paper's dimensional definition
symbols.transition: The final-time surface extrudes backward into the unknown spacetime field.

symbols.visualObject: Propagation-speed dial attached to the PDE operator
symbols.meaning: Wave speed parameter in the stated wave equation
symbols.transition: The dial sets the spatial-curvature contribution to the residual.

symbols.visualObject: Ray from a target spacetime point to source point i
symbols.meaning: Spacetime distance used by radial basis function i
symbols.transition: Each ray collapses into one scalar feature input.

symbols.visualObject: Radial response profile centered at source point i
symbols.meaning: Simplified MQ, IMQ, or Gaussian spacetime radial basis value
symbols.transition: The radial values stack into the MLP input vector.

symbols.visualObject: Residual marker at an interior spacetime point
symbols.meaning: Mismatch between the predicted field and the wave equation
symbols.transition: Residual markers feed the physics-loss channel.

symbols.meaning: Training objective combining observation mismatch and PDE residual
symbols.transition: The combined loss contracts as network parameters update.

symbols.meaning: Noise level used in the paper's contaminated-data experiments
symbols.transition: Activating the toggle perturbs observations and updates the error maps.

return.visualObject: The original star-shaped domain showing the analytical and reconstructed wave surfaces with an adjacent absolute-error field
return.claim: For the tested synthetic inverse cases, the optimized spacetime IMQ RBF-PINN reconstructs the wave field with reported high accuracy despite partial conditions and s = 0.1 noise.
return.claimBoundary: The conclusion is limited to the prescribed two-dimensional geometry, analytical solution, point distributions, network search, and artificial noise experiment; it does not establish performance on unmodeled field physics or arbitrary missing-data patterns.
return.sourceExcerpt: Notably, this study presents a robust PINN capable of accurately solving inverse wave problems, even with incomplete boundary data with noise contamination. In the absence of noise, the model achieves RMSE values of 10−6 for Case A and Case B, while Case C and Case D, which exclude initial conditions and include only partial boundary conditions, attain RMSE values of 10−4 and 10−5, respectively. When subjected to a noise level of 0.1, all cases consistently maintain RMSE values of 10−4.

visual.primaryObject: A wave surface evolving through an extruded star-shaped spacetime domain
visual.accentObject: RBF source centers and physics-residual points
visual.measuredQuantity: Wave function u and its absolute reconstruction error
visual.geometryNotes: Begin with the paper's irregular polar boundary, extrude it along time, map targets to spacetime source centers by r_i, collapse radial shells into an RBF feature layer, and return predictions as spatial wave surfaces for Cases A-D.
